coredata.dc:title: Doable production of highly fluorescent, heteroatom-doped graphene material from fuel coke for cellular bioimaging: An eco-sustainable cradle-to-gate approach
coredata.dc:creator: Thomas, Riya
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: The manifold usage of fluorescent materials and their pliable association with optical imaging techniques have made great strides in unfolding the incredible potential of biotechnological research, particularly in cancer treatment, from point-of-care assay to clinical applications. Enlarged nuclei or numerous counts often indicate tumor growth activity, and these expressions can be visualized with the aid of fluorescence imaging. Therefore, developing highly fluorescent, biocompatible, and sustainable biomarkers for imaging is a vital necessity for their extensive application in cancer diagnosis and therapy. In this work, we have demonstrated the ‘cradle-to-gate’ transformation of abundant and cheap fossil fuel coke into a fluorescent probe for bioimaging. Herein, for the first time, a facile strategy for modulating the emission characteristics of coke-derived graphene system via doping of heteroatoms has been reported. It is found that the doping of nitrogen atoms could strongly influence photophysical properties, giving rise to increased quantum yield (16%), extended fluorescence lifetime (8.51 ns), and higher photostability (92%). Moreover, the as-synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene derivative is used as a potential labelling agent for the cellular imaging of cancerous cells, as well as normal cells, at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL. For 24h incubation, the cells cultured with a concentration of 25 μg/mL were observed to have an appreciable fluorescence accompanied by significant biocompatibility, with a viability value of ≥85%. Considering the heteroatom-induced emission characteristics and bioanalytical acuities, it is prospective that the coke-derived graphene system can be further explored to elucidate its significance in biomedical applications, without compromising on economic and environmental sustainability.

entry.bold: Nitrogen doped coke-based multi-layer graphene derivative

head.ce:title: Doable production of highly fluorescent, heteroatom-doped graphene material from fuel coke for cellular bioimaging: An eco-sustainable cradle-to-gate approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Research Laboratory, Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ University, Bengaluru, 560029, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science Research Laboratory, Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ University, Bengaluru, 560029, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The manifold usage of fluorescent materials and their pliable association with optical imaging techniques have made great strides in unfolding the incredible potential of biotechnological research, particularly in cancer treatment, from point-of-care assay to clinical applications. Enlarged nuclei or numerous counts often indicate tumor growth activity, and these expressions can be visualized with the aid of fluorescence imaging. Therefore, developing highly fluorescent, biocompatible, and sustainable biomarkers for imaging is a vital necessity for their extensive application in cancer diagnosis and therapy. In this work, we have demonstrated the ‘cradle-to-gate’ transformation of abundant and cheap fossil fuel coke into a fluorescent probe for bioimaging. Herein, for the first time, a facile strategy for modulating the emission characteristics of coke-derived graphene system via doping of heteroatoms has been reported. It is found that the doping of nitrogen atoms could strongly influence photophysical properties, giving rise to increased quantum yield (16%), extended fluorescence lifetime (8.51 ns), and higher photostability (92%). Moreover, the as-synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene derivative is used as a potential labelling agent for the cellular imaging of cancerous cells, as well as normal cells, at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL. For 24h incubation, the cells cultured with a concentration of 25 μg/mL were observed to have an appreciable fluorescence accompanied by significant biocompatibility, with a viability value of ≥85%. Considering the heteroatom-induced emission characteristics and bioanalytical acuities, it is prospective that the coke-derived graphene system can be further explored to elucidate its significance in biomedical applications, without compromising on economic and environmental sustainability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the arena of biological and biomedical science, fluorophores (or fluorescent materials) have gained significant attention by providing a comprehensive understanding of physiological activity and cellular interactions through simple pathway-based evaluations. The fluorophores are being used as indicators, probes, labels, and traces to scrutinize bio-systems for academic research and advancing real-life applications (Valeur and Berberan-Santos, 2013; Wolfbeis et al., 2002). The combination of fluorescence traits along with optical imaging techniques can provide new directions to get within fundamental biological limits. With this versatile combination, fluorescence imaging technique facilitates the visualization of sub-cellular structures, activity of metabolites, and characterization of molecular mechanisms that influence genetic processes in living organisms (Chao et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2014). Besides, it prompts many possibilities for advancements in the field of clinical medicine, especially those related to early disease diagnosis, drug development, and therapeutic responses.

In recent years, the incidence of melanoma, a highly resistant and malignant skin cancer, has increased tremendously, compared to other types of cancer. Though it is only 4% out of all skin cancer cases, it is the most antagonistic and leads to 80% of deaths (Apalla et al., 2017). So, the early detection of melanoma is a vital medical necessity, as it can facilitate the complete cure of patients through simple surgical excision or treatments. However, the commonly used clinical tools for cancer diagnosis and prognosis are deficient in offering consistent and precise information, owing to their low sensitivity and limited spatial resolution (Foster et al., 2008; McCormack et al., 2011). But fluorescence has the advantage of being highly sensitive, flexible, and quickly responsive, allowing it to have spatial resolution down to a few nanometers. Accordingly, fluorescence imaging has proved to be an effective and efficient technique in cancer theranostics (diagnosis and therapy) due to its ability to detect cancer at primary stages and assess tumor activity with higher detection accuracy.

Fluorescence imaging enables the acquisition of information of biological interest at cellular or molecular levels by utilizing various wavelengths emitted by fluorescent materials. Organic dyes, synthetic fluorophores, and metal-based semiconductor quantum dots are conventionally used imaging probes or contrast agents in bioimaging (Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Mohandoss et al., 2020). Although exceptional in fluorescence characteristics, they are highly toxic due to the involvement of heavy metals in synthesis and involve an increased cost of production owing to their expensive precursors, which ultimately hinder their ample utilization in required applications.

Carbon-based nanomaterials could overcome the limitations of these traditional fluorophores in relation to their tunable emission, photostability, biocompatibility, solubility in water, and ease of functionalization (Wang and Lu, 2022; Yang et al., 2022). Compared with other forms of carbon materials, graphene, the two-dimensional nanomaterial, has got superior physiochemical characteristics required by a biomarker for capturing explicable images of biological matter (Shen et al., 2012). Particularly, hydrogen bonding between the graphene surface and oxygen functional groups observed in single or multilayer graphene oxide exerts excellent surface reactivity that can contribute to the adsorptive capability of drugs or biomolecules and growth aspects beneficial for in-vivo or in-vitro cellular imaging (Nishida et al., 2016). Their tunable fluorescence and high light-emitting efficiency originating from the edge effects of oxygen functional groups are indeed attractive for bioimaging. However, when it comes to practical application, the costly graphene precursor, tedious synthesis route, and intricate mode of chemical functionalization are not environmentally sustainable. Under this context, contemporary research efforts are shifting toward naturally available carbonaceous precursors and facile reaction processes for developing novel materials, thus contributing to the advancement of bio-economy. Especially, the consumption of carbon-based waste materials or other low-cost industrial sources for the synthesis of graphene derivatives facilitates their sustainable utilization and lowered impact on the planet.

Coal is one of the most plentiful sources of carbon, with inherent graphitic planes that can be used to extract graphene derivatives. The composition of carbon precursors is always a crucial factor that determines the quality of ensuing graphene-like material. The high-temperature carbonization of coal produces coke, having a high content of carbon, rarer impurities, and a uniquely layered structure. Thus, the utilization of coke to synthesize graphene derivatives will be a value-addition to the pollution-causing fossil fuel coal. The luminescent versatilities of as-derived graphene systems could be further enhanced by several means, such as functionalization, doping and surface passivation to extend their application in bioimaging (Georgakilas et al., 2012; Mohandoss et al., 2021). Recently, a multitude of papers has reported that the doping of heteroatoms can endow graphene systems with tailored fluorescence as well as other photophysical properties (Wang et al., 2014; Das et al., 2019). Nitrogen is the most eminent doping candidate for the modulation of emission characteristics, while phosphorus is also used in combination with it (Liu et al., 2012). The doping of N or P could increase the electron density and alter the electronic environment, ultimately resulting in improved fluorescent properties (Paraknowitsch et al., 2013). However, there are no prior reports on the synthesis of N and P doped coke-derived graphene structures or the description of their heteroatom-induced emission characteristics that lay the foundation for their potential application in cellular imaging.

This study mainly focuses on establishing a facile, low-cost, and sustainable methodology for synthesizing highly-fluorescent heteroatom-doped, coke-based graphene derivatives. With regard to the superior fluorescence characteristics and appreciable cell viability, nitrogen-doped graphene derivative (N-GO) was applied as a biomarker for confocal imaging of both cancerous (melanoma) and normal (RAW) cells. This work aims to create a perception about the amazing optical properties of coke by generating a value-added and sustainable fluorescent probe that can be used as a stepping stone for further research in biological assays.

All the chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade. Phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 wt %), and ethylenediamine (NH2CH2CH2NH2), were obtained from s d fine chemicals, India. Deionized and doubly distilled water was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of heteroatom-doped coke-based multilayer graphene derivative
ce:section.ce:para: Improved Hummers’ method was used to extract oxidized multilayer graphene derivative (GO) from fossil fuel coke as reported in our prior work (Thomas and Balachandran, 2021). The heteroatom-doped samples were prepared via a simple, facile, and one-pot hydrothermal technique. Briefly, the coke-derived GO solution (2 mg/ml) was magnetically stirred with 2.5 ml of phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and 3.5 ml of ethylenediamine, followed by ultrasonication for 1 h at 40 °C. The reaction mixture was then transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the solution was centrifuged and washed several times with distilled water. Finally, it was vacuum dried at 80 °C overnight, and the obtained co-doped sample was labelled as NP-GO. A similar procedure was followed to prepare phosphorus-doped (P-GO) and nitrogen-doped (N-GO) multilayer graphene derivatives using 5 ml of phosphoric acid and 7 ml of ethylenediamine as phosphorus and nitrogen sources, respectively. For comparison, the undoped sample was prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of GO solution without any dopants. The schematics for the synthesis procedure and dopant configurations of as-synthesized heteroatom-doped coke-based multilayer graphene derivatives are shown in Fig. 1 .

The crystalline phases of the samples mentioned above (Section 2.2) were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku 6000) with Cu kα as the radiation source. The structural information was described by an inVia Raman Microscope at 514 nm laser excitation wavelength from 500 to 3500 cm−1. The topographic features were recorded using Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM Jeol 2100, 200 kV). Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and elemental mapping analysis were performed on Gemini SEM-300. The surface chemistry characteristics were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Omicron ESCA probe, Mg Kα X rays). The emission properties were recorded via a Shimadzu fluoro-spectrometer (RF-5301). The fluorescence decay curve was measured using the TCSPC capability of HORIBA Fluorolog FL-1057 spectrometer.

RAW cell line (human macrophage cell line) and melanoma cell line (human skin cancer line) were procured from the National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM-Himedia), supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic cocktail containing Penicillin (100U/ml), Streptomycin (100 μg/ml), and Amphotericin B (2.5 μg/ml). The cell containing TC flasks (25 cm2) were incubated at 37 °C at 5% CO2 environment with humidity in a cell culture incubator (Galaxy® 170 Eppendorf, Germany).

Cells (2500 cells/well) were seeded on 96 well plates and allowed to acclimatize to the culture conditions, such as 37 °C and 5% CO2 environment in the incubator for 24 h. The heteroatom-doped sample (N-GO) was prepared in DMEM media (100 mg/mL) and filtered using a 0.2 μm Millipore syringe filter. The samples were further diluted in DMEM media and added to the wells containing cultured cells at final concentrations of 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL respectively and untreated wells were kept as control. After treatment with the test samples, the plates were further incubated for 24 h. After the incubation period, the media from the wells were aspirated, discarded, and subjected to fluorescence analysis under the blue filter of an inverted phase contrast fluorescence microscope at 20× magnification (Labomed TCM 400, USA).

The MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was used to measure cellular metabolic activity as an indicator of cell viability, proliferation, and cytotoxicity. The cells were cultured on 96 well plates with a density of 2500 cells/well and incubated with different concentrations (6.25–100 μg/mL) of heteroatom doped sample (N-GO) at 37 °C and 5% CO2 environment in the incubator for 24 h. The test samples were prepared in DMEM media (100 mg/mL) and filtered with a sterilized 0.2 μm Millipore syringe filter. Untreated wells were kept as control. After the incubation period, the media from the wells were aspirated and discarded. 100 μL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution in PBS was added to the wells. The plates were further incubated for 2 h to develop formazan crystals. The supernatant was removed, and 100 μL DMSO (100%) was added per well. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured with a microplate reader. Two wells per plate without cells served as blanks. All the experiments were conducted in triplicates.

The cell viability was expressed using the following equation (1):

To comprehend the variation in the crystal structure of coke-derived multilayer graphene system upon doping of heteroatoms, an XRD study was performed and the results are shown in Fig. 2 a. The peak attributing to the characteristic graphitic crystal plane (002) was observed at ∼ 25° in XRD patterns of all the samples, whereas the peak centered at 41° having lower intensity was ascribed to the reflection of (100) plane (JCPDS file 75–2078) (Chen and Teik, 2019). The XRD patterns of doped samples (P-GO, N-GO, NP-GO) were similar to those of undoped GO, signifying that the doping exerted negligible impact on the crystalline structure of the graphene system. The interlayer spacing of GO, P-GO, N-GO, and NP-GO calculated from (002) diffraction peak was found to be 0.351 nm, 0.355 nm, 0.344 nm, and 0.349 nm, respectively. The increased interlayer spacing or the shifting of diffraction peak to a lower 2θ degree observed in P-GO is due to the introduction of oxygen functional groups from the phosphorus precursor, H3PO4.

The structural deformations and defect densities were further investigated by Raman analysis. As seen in Fig. 2b, the principal characteristics of carbon samples, such as D, G, 2D, and D + G bands, were observed in the wavelength region of 1000–3000 cm−1. It should be noted that the D-band corresponded to structural disorders, and defects were observed around 1350 cm−1; G-band corresponded to sp2 crystalline, amorphous carbon appeared at about 1580 cm−1; 2D band due to active defects was observed around 2700 cm−1 and the D + G band was occurring around 2930 cm−1 (Lee and Mahajan, 2021). However, a slight, blue shift was observed for the G-band in doped samples owing to the alterations in electronic properties and bonding configurations caused by doping effects (Matsoso et al., 2016). Oxygen functional introduced into the graphene lattice from the phosphorus precursor (H3PO4) prevented the stacking of the layer, and hence relatively high-intense 2D bands were witnessed for P-GO and NP-GO. Meanwhile, an increment in the intensity of D-band and a decrement in the intensity of 2D-band observed for N-GO can be assigned to the creation of more defects. The parameter used for the quantification of defect densities was ID/IG (intensity of D-band to intensity to G-band) values. The ID/IG ratio of GO, P-GO, N-GO, and NP-GO were calculated to be 0.84, 0.87, 0.96, and 0.89, respectively. The higher ID/IG values of doped samples indicated the replacement of C atoms by N and P, confirming a successful doping and maximum incidence of defects.

The morphology of as-synthesized samples was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As evident in Fig. 3 , all the samples possessed an ordered sheet-like multilayered structure. The darker areas corresponded to the regions with folding and overlapping of the sheets (Wilson et al., 2009). Compared with pristine GO, the doped system had higher folds and crimples due to the inclusion of heteroatoms, which caused more defects on the surface and edges of the graphene structure. The HR-TEM images displaying lattice fringes and the correponding value of lattice spacing were provided in Fig. 3(e–h).

The chemical structures of all the samples were examined by employing FTIR spectroscopy, and the results are depicted in Fig. 4 . The characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to oxygen functionalities and N/P heteroatoms were observed in the FTIR spectra of GO, P-GO, N-GO, and NP-GO. The broad, absorption peak around 3400 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of –OH groups or the trapped water molecules. Peaks at 1710, 1572, 1180, and 1050 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO bond, skeleton vibrations of graphene domains (CC), stretching of C–O bond, and presence of C–OH stretching, respectively (Ramya et al., 2021). It was obvious that the intensity of bands due to the oxygen functionalities (C–O, CO, C–OH) declined in N-GO and NP-GO, which were caused by the fact that the samples were deoxygenated due to the reduction effect of nitrogen precursor, ethylenediamine. The broad peak at 1175 cm−1 may be assigned to the integrated vibrations of C–O, C–P, or C–N bond (Mu et al., 2016). Interestingly, along with the characteristic vibrations of C–P, C–N, and P–O in the spectrum of NP-GO, an additional peak marked at 730 cm−1 indicated the occurrence of N–P bond (Valappil et al., 2018). Hence, the FTIR spectra confirmed the presence of heteroatoms, signifying the successful doping of nitrogen and phosphorus in the graphene network.

The energy dispersive (EDX) spectra shown in Fig. 5 a-d disclose the presence of heteroatoms in doped samples without any trace of impurity, signifying that the synthesized samples were of high purity. The elemental mapping images (Fig. 5f) portrayed the formation of well-dispersed heteroatom-doped graphene derivative with elements of C, O, P, and N, which was in good agreement with the results of FTIR, XRD, and Raman analysis.

The elemental composition and the nature of chemical bonds in each heteroatom-doped graphene sample have been studied through XPS analysis, and the obtained survey spectra are depicted in Fig. S1. The relative atomic percentages of phosphorus and nitrogen in mono-doped samples were calculated to be 2.89 At% (P-GO) and 5.92 At% (N-GO), respectively. Whereas, the co-doped NP-GO sample showed the presence of both heteroatoms with relative atomic % of P: 1.73 and N: 3.92.

The high-resolution spectra and deconvoluted peaks (bonding components) in the region of C1s and O1s for doped and undoped samples are shown in Fig. 6 . The peak corresponding to sp2 or sp3, carbon at 284.7 eV is the characteristic feature of the C1s region (Fig. 6a). The component at 285.5 ± 0.1 eV is assigned to C atoms bonded to the heteroatoms (N/P) (Liu et al., 2017; Li et al., 2013). The presence of C–P and C–N peaks confirms the successful doping of P and N atoms into the carbon lattice of graphene sheets. The component at 286.1 ± 0.1 eV is assigned to C atoms directly bonded to the oxygen atoms in hydroxyl or epoxy configurations. The smaller components at 287.5 ± 0.3 eV and 288. 9 ± 0.3 eV are related to carbonyl (CO) and carboxyl groups (-O-CO) (Chen et al., 2020). Deconvolution of O1s spectra (part b of Fig. 6) produces 4 main peaks around 530.5 ± 0.2 eV, 531.8 ± 0.2 eV, 533.1 ± 0.3 eV, and 534 ± 0.3 eV corresponding to CO (of carbonyl group), C–O (of epoxy group), C–OH (of hydroxyl group) and -O-CO (of carboxyl group) respectively (Pacheco et al., 2015; Thomas and Balachandran, 2023). The doped samples exhibited 532.5 ± 0.1 eV and 533.7 ± 0.2 eV corresponding to nitric oxide (NOx) and P–O–P groups that have resulted from the intercalation of heteroatoms (Sakulsermsuk et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2013).

The atomic component fraction of each surface functional group has been calculated from the area of the deconvoluted C1s and O1s peaks of the respective samples. The variations in the content of graphitic carbon (area of C–C/CC peak) and oxygenated carbon (sum of CO + CO + -O-CO peaks) in all four samples are presented in Fig. 7 a. It is worth mentioning that the highest fraction of graphitic carbon (80%) is detected in the undoped sample (GO), followed by N-GO (72%), NP-GO (67%), and P-GO (63%). The replacement of carbon atoms by heteroatoms (N/P) and the introduction of oxygen functionalities by phosphoric acid have significantly reduced the graphitic carbon content. Moreover, N-GO comprises the least amount of oxygenated carbon out of all four samples due to the reducing effect induced by ethylenediamine. The XPS spectral analyses of the O1s spectra revealed that the as-synthesized samples possess a carbon network enriched with different oxygen functionalities, and the atomic composition of each of those oxygen moieties is displayed in Fig. 7b. It should be noted that the contribution of C–O and C–OH is more substantial compared to CO and O–CO. In parallel with the main oxygen components, a small contribution of NOx and P–O–P is observed for heteroatom-modified samples. Hence, it is evident from the graphs that the surface atomic content of the doped and undoped samples relies on the nature of the chemical modification involved.

The N1s and P1s spectra are used to elucidate the phosphorus and nitrogen dopant configurations in the graphene lattice. The deconvolution of the high-resolution spectra of P2p shown in Fig. 8 a revealed the presence of core-level peaks at binding energies of 129.5 ± 0.1 eV and 130.4 eV, for elemental phosphorus and graphitic phosphorus, respectively (Yew et al., 2017). The peak observed at 134.7 ± 0.1 eV is due to the P–O of the metaphosphate group (Langer et al., 2020). Compared with N-GO, an additional peak at a binding energy of 132.8 eV is observed in NP-GO, attributed to the N–P bond aroused from the co-doping of heteroatoms (MacIntosh et al., 2018). To further reflect on the environment of the nitrogen atom in mono-doped and co-doped graphene systems, the deconvolution analysis of the core level peaks of N1s spectra is carried out as shown in Fig. 8b. The deconvoluted components observed at binding energies of 398.7 eV and 399.9 ± 0.1 eV, suggest the pyridinic N and graphitic/pyrrolic N doping configurations, respectively. The contribution from N-oxide is witnessed at the binding energy of 401.8 eV (Figueras et al., 2019). Also, a small peak is observed at 401.1 eV in N1s spectra of NP-GO, which is associated with the bonding of nitrogen with phosphorus to form N–P signatures (Li et al., 2017). Thus, the configuration of heteroatoms in the graphene lattice could be resolved using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, as the difference in binding energy is high enough to distinguish between the adsorptive or substitutional doping.

To unravel the fluorescence emission of the heteroatom-doped graphene system, the emission spectra were obtained for the excitation wavelengths ranging from 240 to 520 nm in intervals of 20 nm. As seen in Fig. 9 , fluorescence intensity initially increases and then decreases with the increment in excitation wavelength. This effect has been observed in nanostructured materials owing to several factors, such as heterogeneous surface states, electronic properties, and varying morphology (Singh et al., 2018; Thomas et al., 2018). For relatively lower wavelengths, the electrons are pumped into higher energy states of the conduction band, which is distant from the radiative electronic states. This significantly drops the photon emission; hence, the fluorescence emission intensity is found to increase with the excitations in shorter wavelength regions. Wherein fewer electrons are pumped into the conduction band for longer wavelength regions, thereby, the emission spectra show a reduction in the fluorescence intensity with an increase in excitation wavelength (Tang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the strong emission bands demonstrated in the fluorescence spectra signify the existence of different emissive centers. The spectra of undoped graphene system display two characteristic bands, which can be attributed to core and surface states. The π-π*charge transfer transitions in the sp2 conjugated carbon domain and the surface functional groups contribute towards these core and surface states, respectively. But, in the heteroatom-doped graphene system, along with these two characteristic bands, an additional spectral band, so-called “edge states,” achieved via n-π* transitions from N, P, or O groups are witnessed (Du and Guo, 2016; Mohandoss et al., 2022).

Indeed, fluorescence spectra also provide definitive evidence for the involvement of excitation wavelength independent/dependent emissions. The intrinsic luminescence from sp2 conjugated carbon network contribute to the excitation-independent emission at lower wavelength regions (Thomas and Balachandran, 2021). Meanwhile, the doping of N and P species allow the redshifting of emission characteristics and cause the formation of another excitation-independent emissive core trap state. There is, however, a verge trend: shift in emission peak position for the excitation wavelengths beyond a specific range from 380 to 400 nm (UV to near visible region). The reason for this peculiar excitation-dependent emission is grounded in the existence of several kinds of chromophore/fluorophores, which are nothing but the aromatic and oxidation groups in the GO structure (Mohandoss et al., 2021a,b). The various oxygen-containing functional groups produce multiple energy levels in the n-π* gap, and hence make the fluorescence emission wavelength dependent. The quantum chemical calculations have also suggested the creation of emissive electronic states via distortion of the electronic environment induced by surface oxidation of sp2 carbon atoms in the model graphene oxide system (Chien et al., 2012).

The fluorescence spectra consisting of two or three overlapping characteristic bands can be deconvoluted into gaussian-like emission peaks, which comprise excitation-independent and excitation-dependent emission peaks. The curve fitting analysis for doped (P-GO, N-GO, NP-GO) and undoped samples (GO) was obtained for emission spectra measured at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. As seen in Fig. 10 , all the samples exhibited core bands with emission peaks centered at 375 nm, 353 nm, 384 nm, and 369 nm, for GO, P-GO, N-GO, and NP-GO respectively. Similarly, the fluorescence bands owing to the surface functional groups that are primarily excitation dependent are also spotted. However, in doped samples, the emissions from edge bands provide evidence for the existence of heteroatom-related emissive centers. The fluorescence spectra of P-GO, N-GO and NP-GO display an emission maximum at 424 nm, 440 nm and 427 nm, respectively. Wherein, N-GO and NP-GO possess two additional edge-state emissions, at 411 nm and 398 nm, respectively, which are caused by the sub-band gaps within the edge. It is also observed that the intensity of core bands declined with the introduction of heteroatoms, implying that edge bands are the most domineering emissive component in doped samples. Furthermore, the blueshift or redshift observed in the core, edge, and surface state emissions is caused by the alterations in defects or surface functional groups induced by doping of heteroatoms. Compared to GO (375 nm), the core band of P-GO exhibited a blueshift (353 nm), while N-GO showed a redshift (384 nm). The redshift is likely to be caused by the increased electron density aroused from the doping of N atoms in the form of graphitic N and the reduction of oxygen functional groups by the N-precursor (ethylenediamine), leading to the narrowing of bandgap and decreased transition energy (Yang et al., 2018). These findings are consistent with the absorption spectra and energy band gap obtained from the Tauc plot (Fig. S2).

The CIE (Commission Internationale de l’Elcairage) chromaticity diagrams of all the samples are drawn from the fluorescence spectra data. As seen in Fig. 11 , emission colors ranging from blue to green-yellow are observed in each sample for increasing excitation wavelengths, implying their potential applications in fluorescence sensing, bio-imaging, and optoelectronics.

Fluorescence lifetime measurements are carried out using the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique and the decay profiles are depicted in Fig. 12 . The fluorescence decay profile exhibited multi-exponential behavior and the emission decay is best fitted using a triple exponential function. The average lifetime of undoped GO is calculated to be 2.67 ns. After phosphorus doping, the fluorescence decay is found to be suppressed and the corresponding fluorescence lifetime is 0.36 ns. In the case of nitrogen doping, calculated fluorescence lifetime is 8.51 ns, which is much higher than that of the phosphorus-doped or co-doped (NP-GO: 5.95 ns) graphene system.

For a comprehensive analysis, the radiative and non-radiative decay rates of all the samples are estimated using the following equations (2) and (3), and the results are summarized in Table 1 ;

Where,

are quantum yield (Q.Y.), and average lifetime of the sample, respectively (Barman et al., 2014).

The fluorescence Q.Y. of GO, P-GO, N-GO, and NP-GO is determined to be around 8–16%. The largest change in Q.Y. is found in nitrogen-doped sample, where the Q.Y. is increased by 1.6-fold more than that of the undoped sample. The calculated average lifetime is found to be consistent with the fluorescence Q.Y. for all the samples. The correlated data reveal that the long fluorescence lifetime observed for N-GO is attributable to its high Q.Y. along with the delayed radiative decay rate, wherein in P-GO the non-radiative relaxation pathway is found to be more favorable, which in turn lessened its fluorescence lifetime and Q.Y. Neither phosphorus doping, nor co-doping could facilitate the enhancement of fluorescence Q.Y.

The surface functional groups attached to the edges of the graphene lattice significantly influence the photophysical characteristics (Tang et al., 2019). The C–O/C–OH groups can synergistically hybridize into sp2 conjugated π-domains to form a fluorophore, while -O-CO/COOH groups act as a non-radiative recombination center. This finding corresponds to the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and O1s (Figs. 6 and 7) for GO, P-GO, N-GO, and NP-GO. The Q.Y. followed a reverse trend with the peak height of the -O-CO group. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that the fluorescence Q.Y. is critically affected by epoxy/hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups.

To authenticate the photostability of as-synthesized heteroatom doped graphene derivatives, each sample was exposed to illumination (∼350 nm) for 5 h, and only minimal photobleaching was witnessed (<8%) in the fluorescence emission profile (Fig. S3). Explicitly, the fluorescence properties are analyzed after one month of storage, and it has been found that the emission maintained its stability as well as intensity. The optical analysis of the heteroatom-doped graphene derivative reveals that nitrogen doping could significantly enhance the fluorescence properties, and consequently, its potential application in cellular bioimaging is inferred.

The fluorescence bioimaging application of as-synthesized N-GO was evaluated for both normal and cancerous cell lines. The confocal microscopic images of RAW and melanoma cells after incubation with N-GO obtained using the excitation filters of 450–480 nm are shown in Fig. 13 .

As seen in the figure, the fluorescent heteroatom-doped graphene could easily enter the cell and exhibit a green, fluorescence image in sight. Further, a concentration-dependent fluorescence is observed for the incubation of 0–100 μg/ml of N-GO with RAW and melanoma cells. It is the 100 μg/ml of N-GO that shows bright fluorescence due to its high penetration into the cell lines. The results mentioned above portray that the coke-derived N-GO can potentially be valued in in-vitro cellular imaging. A comparison of the developed biomarker with existing techniques towards the detection of melanoma cell lines are given in Table 2 . The results show that the heteroatom-doped coke-based biomarkers could achieve better excellent fluorescent characteristics than most of the reported works. It also facilitates the detection and visualization of molecular processes via a simplified operation technique, without causing any physical or biological interference.

The cytotoxicity of the biomarker is a vital parameter for its applicability in the bioimaging of cells. Hence, the cytotoxicity of N-GO is evaluated by means of an MTT assay for RAW and melanoma cells. The cell viability evaluation of both cells incubated with an increasing concentration of N-GO is displayed in Fig. 14 . The cell viability is ∼85%, even after 24 h incubation for concentrations of 6.25–25 μg/mL. Thus, the above results portray the biocompatibility of as-synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene derivatives, especially in lower concentrations for biological applications.

The emission characteristics and cell viability of graphene systems are subjected to factors such as size, synthesis route, surface functionalities, dosage amount, and exposure time. Therefore, the inflection over these environmental transformation-induced property changes can further improve their performance in biological responses.

In summary, the present study reports the value-added production of highly fluorescent heteroatom-doped graphene-based material from coke as an approach to reduce the environmental damage caused by fossil fuels. The doping of heteroatoms was achieved with the aid of a simple one-pot hydrothermal methodology using ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid as nitrogen and phosphorus precursors, respectively. Compared with phosphorus, the substitution of carbon by nitrogen atoms significantly amended the photophysical characteristics with an increase in quantum yield from 10 to 16%. Thus, the as-synthesized nitrogen-doped coke-derived graphene structures (N-GO) is used as a biomarker for in-vitro imaging applications. Inspired by its remarkable fluorescence emission, N-GO could easily facilitate the labelling of human macrophage cell lines and human skin cancer cell lines. Also, cytotoxicity of the biomarker is considered and found to be efficient, especially in lower concentrations. Accordingly, our proposed stratagem provides new insights into the cellular bioimaging applications of fossil-fuel derived graphene structures, which can be a pioneer and sustainable contender to those conventional fluoroprobes.
